title: Chapter 1: The Choice Paralysis
content: There are seventeen unread messages on my phone and every single one of them wants me to decide who I am.

I'm sitting on the bathroom floor of my Princeton dorm—at least, I think I'm sitting. It's hard to tell when your body has turned into a question mark. Knees up, head down, paper bag clutched to my face like it's a lifeline and not something that used to hold a mediocre bagel from the dining hall. The tile is ice against my bare feet, the kind of cold that seeps up through your soles and into your bones until you forget what warmth felt like. The fluorescent light above the mirror hums its industrial dirge—bzzzzt, bzzzzt—like a bee that gave up on living but couldn't stop vibrating.

I breathe into the bag. In, out. The paper tastes salty—sweat from my own palms, probably, or maybe the bag absorbed my tears. Salty paper, which sounds like the world's worst hors d'oeuvre. I'd laugh if I weren't busy hyperventilating.

It's 2 AM. Graduation was today. Sorry, *yesterday* now. The ceremony happened, the caps flew, the photos were taken, and somewhere between the dean's handshake and the champagne toast I apparently lost the ability to function as a person. I've been in this bathroom for—how long? An hour? Three? The shower curtain keeps looking at me funny.

The smell of the dining hall's institutional hand soap—pink, slimy, vaguely chemical—drifts from the dispenser on the wall. It mingles with the sourness of the bag and something else, something underneath it all: the staleness of a room that hasn't been cleaned properly since spring break. My bathroom. My last night in this bathroom, and I'm spending it on the floor like a feral animal.

My phone screen is on. It's been on. The brightness cuts through the bathroom's terrible lighting, and I can see the emails without even picking it up. Three of them. Three life sentences dressed as opportunities.

Goldman Sachs. The subject line is still there: *Offer Extension — Final Deadline Tomorrow*. New York City. A salary that would make my mother cry with something that isn't quite pride but looks like it from a distance. A corner office eventually, maybe, after I sacrifice enough of my soul to the altar of quarterly earnings. A life that looks like a highlight reel if you don't look too hard at the seams.

The Sorbonne. *Félicitations! Votre admission au programme de master…* Paris. A master's in comparative literature that I applied for on a dare from myself, because sometimes the most dangerous dares are the ones where nobody else is watching. Cobblestones and café au lait and a language I'd have to learn properly, finally, after years of casually pretending Duolingo counted.

And the third one. The one that isn't even an email. It's sitting on the counter above me, a real letter with a real wax seal—crimson, the kind you see in period dramas about duchesses. It arrived three days ago by actual physical mail, like it was 1847, from a woman named Siobhan who runs a cat café in Galway, Ireland, and who had apparently read a blog post I wrote junior year about wanting a "life reset" and decided I was serious. The letter offers a room above the café, a small stipend, and all the cats I could ever need. She called it a "soft place to land."
A soft place to land. As if I were falling.

I look up at the mirror from my position on the floor, and the face looking back is a stranger's. Perfect skin—thank you, genetics and a seventeen-step Korean skincare routine I maintained with religious devotion. Perfect hair, even at 2 AM, even after crying into a bag. Perfect grades—summa cum laude, baby, the kind of Latin that makes parents beam at dinner parties. But the eyes in that reflection are empty. Not sad, exactly. Just hollow. Like someone renovated the apartment and forgot to move in.
Perfect nothing inside.
I was eight years old the first time choice broke me.
I'm not being dramatic. Okay, I'm being a little dramatic, but it's *my* trauma and I'll dramatize if I want to.

The cereal aisle. ShopRite, Hamilton, New Jersey, on a Saturday morning. The fluorescent lights were just as buzzy and desperate as the ones above me now. My mother was pushing the cart two aisles ahead—she'd given up waiting for me approximately forty-five seconds after we entered the cereal section, because even then, even at eight, my decision-making process ran on geological time.

I stood there, transfixed. The entire wall of boxes was a mosaic of possibility. Cocoa Puffs on the left, that rich chocolate promise. Froot Loops on the right, technicolor and chaotic. And between them: Cheerios, Frosted Flakes, Cinnamon Toast Crunch, Rice Krispies that talked when you poured the milk, and a hundred other doors to a hundred other breakfasts, and I could only walk through one.

So I stood there. And I stood there. And then I was crying—not loud, not a tantrum, just tears sliding down my face in quiet, helpless streaks. Because picking one meant killing all the others. Cocoa Puffs meant no Froot Loops. Froot Loops meant no Cocoa Puffs. And every other box was a ghost of a morning I'd never have.

My mother found me like that. Her sigh was its own kind of weather system—impatient, exhausted, the sound of a woman who had work at six and three kids and no time for existential crises in the cereal aisle.
"Just pick one, Avery."

She said it the way you'd say *just breathe* or *just walk*—like it was simple, like it was a thing people did, picking one thing and letting the others go. Like there wasn't a whole multiverse of forgotten breakfasts collapsing behind every decision.

I picked the Cocoa Puffs. I ate them. They were fine. They were just fine. And every morning for two weeks, while the Cocoa Puffs got progressively stalder in their box, I thought about the Froot Loops.

That's the thing nobody tells you about FOMO. It's not a personality quirk. It's not a hashtag. It's a wound, is what it is. A lifelong, slowly bleeding wound masquerading as an aesthetic. And no amount of therapy jargon or mindfulness apps has ever stitched it shut.
I put the bag down. Breathe. The bathroom air tastes like pink soap and regret.
My phone buzzes. A text notification slides down from the top of the screen.
**Mika:** BIIIIITCH did you accept yet???

I stare at it. Mika Torres, my roommate of four years, my emergency contact, the only person who has seen me cry more times than my mother, is across campus at the post-graduation rager that I left an hour into because the music was too loud and someone kept asking me what my "five-year plan" was and I almost committed a crime.

I don't text back. What would I say? *No, Mika, I haven't accepted, because accepting one means rejecting two, and I have spent my entire life building a self out of keeping options open and I don't know how to close a door without feeling like I'm locking myself out?*
That's too long for a text.

I pick up the phone. The screen is warm against my fingers, and I can feel the trembling in my hands—I've been shaking for hours, a fine, constant vibration, like the fluorescent light and I have finally synchronized.

I open the Goldman Sachs email. The words blur and refocus. *We are pleased to extend…* My thumb finds the Accept button. Hovers there. The pad of my fingertip is a millimeter from glass. One tap and I'm a banker. One tap and I have a plan. One tap and my mother gets her highlight reel.
My hand shakes harder.
I close the email.

Open the Sorbonne email. Skim the French, catch every third word because my brain is running on fumes. *Programme de master… vie étudiante…* Paris. God, Paris. The city of people who probably also can't choose their cereal but make it look existential instead of pathological.
I close it.

I turn my head toward the counter, where the letter from Galway sits untouched since I propped it against the soap dispenser. The wax seal is still intact. I haven't opened it—not because I don't want to read it again, but because opening it feels like a commitment. Like I'd be letting Siobhan and her cats and the west coast of Ireland down if I read her words and then chose something else.

I reach up and grab the letter. The paper is thick, expensive, the kind of paper that says *I took my time with this.* The wax seal is warm—I must have been holding it earlier without remembering. I set it on my lap and stare at the crimson wax, and something cracks open in my chest.
"Cat café," I say out loud. My voice is a wreck—raspy from the bag, from the crying, from the silence. "That's what I've become."

I laugh. It comes out wrong, wet and broken, and then I'm crying again, because it's 2 AM and I'm on a bathroom floor and the best option on my horizon might be feeding cats in Ireland and there's something so pathetic about it that it loops all the way back around to funny. A cat café. A *life reset.* As if life came with a reset button. As if I could just hold down the power switch and start over.
As if.

I drop the phone. I don't mean to—my hands just stop cooperating, like my body has vetoed my brain's last remaining executive function. The phone clatters against the tile and lands face-up, screen still glowing, and I let my head fall back against the vanity cabinet and close my eyes.
The fluorescent light hums its monotonous hymn. The cold seeps. The soap smells. I exist.
After a while—not long, maybe thirty seconds, maybe five minutes—I open my eyes and look down at the phone.
And the screen is wrong.

There's an icon on my home screen that wasn't there before. I know every pixel of this phone's display. I arranged the apps myself, color-coded, alphabetized, a small rectangle of control in an uncontrollable world. And there, on the second page, in the spot where my calculator app should be, is something new.

A black icon. Not dark gray, not navy—*black*, the black of a screen with the brightness at zero. In its center, rendered in negative space, is a fork in a road. Two paths diverging. No name beneath it. No label. No app badge. Just black square, white fork, and a silence that feels heavier than an app icon has any right to feel.
I didn't download this.

I sit up straighter. The paper bag crinkles and falls off my lap. My feet are numb from the cold tile, and I shift my weight, pins and needles blooming as circulation returns. I pick up the phone with both hands, holding it like a small, potentially explosive animal.

The icon doesn't move. Doesn't pulse. Doesn't do any of the things a suspicious app might do to lure you in. It just sits there, quiet and certain, like it's always been there and I'm only just noticing.

I swipe to check my other pages. Calculator is on page four now, displaced, shuffled aside. Everything else is the same. Everything else is exactly where I left it, except for this one intrusion, this one impossible square of void.
I go back to page two. The black icon waits.

My thumb moves toward it. Another choice. Tap or don't tap. Open or don't open. And even though every fiber of my decision-avoidant being is screaming at me to put the phone down and walk away, my thumb keeps drifting, pulled by something I don't have a word for. Curiosity is too small. Need is too desperate. It's more like recognition—the feeling of encountering something that was already inside you, waiting to be named.
I tap.
The screen goes white.
Not the blue-white of a loading screen. Not the harsh glare of a static page. This is a warm, enveloping white, like opening your eyes in a snowbank, like the first morning of the world.
Then text appears. One line at a time, as if someone is typing it in real time, as if someone is on the other side of the glass, watching me read:
*You don't have to choose.*
The words hang there, and my chest does something it hasn't done in years—it *lifts*. Just a fraction. Just enough to notice.
*Every path can be walked.*

The bathroom feels different now. The air is charged, electric, like the moment before lightning strikes—when the hairs on your arms rise and the world goes very, very still. The fluorescent hum fades. The smell of soap fades. The cold against my feet fades. There is only the screen, only the words, only the impossible promise.
*Touch to begin.*

Three lines. That's it. No terms and conditions. No privacy policy. No request for my Apple ID or my location or my mother's maiden name. Just three lines that have no business existing and even less business making sense, and yet—
And yet they do. They make *perfect* sense, in the way that a key makes sense when you've been turning doorknobs your whole life and finally, finally found the lock.

My finger hovers over the screen. The last time I was this close to choosing something, I was eight years old in a cereal aisle, paralyzed by abundance, terrified that every yes was a cemetery of nos.
But this is different. This is *every* yes. Every path. Every cereal box in the aisle, all at once, none of them sacrificed.

My hand has stopped shaking. I don't know when that happened, but it's true—my fingers are steady for the first time in hours, maybe months, maybe years. Steady and certain in a way that should scare me, because certainty is a country I've never held a passport for.
The air crackles. The screen glows. The fork in the road on the black icon was a promise, and for the first time in my life, I believe one.
I touch the screen.
The world dissolves.

It doesn't fade or blur or pixelate. It *dissolves*—like sugar in water, like a name you're trying to remember, like the last note of a song that you know was beautiful but can't quite hold. The bathroom sink vanishes. The shower curtain disappears. The fluorescent light winks out. And the mirror—last of all, as if it needs me to see this one final thing—my reflection stares back at me. Eyes wide. Mouth open. Hand outstretched, reaching for something that isn't there anymore.
Then the mirror goes too.

There's a moment—not black, not white, but *between*—a space that exists outside of spaces, where I am no one and everyone and the cereal aisle and the bathroom and Paris and New York and Galway all at once, compressed into a single point of impossible density, and I would scream but I don't have a mouth—
And then it breaks.
I open my eyes.

The bathroom is gone. The dorm is gone. The fluorescent hum is gone, replaced by a silence so clean it feels like the first silence that ever was. I'm lying on my back in sheets that slide against my skin like cool water—silk, actual silk, the kind that costs more than my semester's textbook budget. They smell like lavender and something older, something like library books and rain on stone.
My feet are warm.

I turn my head, and there are windows—floor-to-ceiling windows that stretch from the bed to a ceiling I can't see the top of, and beyond them, filling the entire frame like a painting that's too beautiful to be real, is the Eiffel Tower.

It glitters. Thousands of lights, gold and white, pulsing gently against a sky that can't decide if it's night or morning—deep blue bleeding into violet bleeding into the palest peach at the horizon. Dawn in Paris. The City of Light living up to its name.
I'm in Paris.
I'm in *Paris*.
The silk sheets. The windows. The Tower. The impossible, unmistakable, irreducible fact of Paris existing outside the email I couldn't bring myself to accept.

My hand moves to my face, and there's no paper bag. There's no bag. There's no bathroom. There's just me, Avery Chen, lying in a bed I've never seen, in a city I've never visited, wearing a silk camisole I definitely don't own, and through the windows the Eiffel Tower keeps glittering like it's been waiting for me.
I touched the screen. Every path can be walked.

And somewhere—somewhere else, somewhere that might as well be a dream now—a girl who looks just like me is still sitting on a bathroom floor, staring at a home screen that no longer has a black icon on it.
But that's not my problem anymore.

I sit up, and Paris pours through the windows like light through a cathedral, and my heart is doing something it hasn't done since I was eight years old and the world was still full of cereal aisles and unlimited mornings.
It's beating fast, but not from fear.
From wonder.
